sets.title: Accusative vs Dative with Two-Way Prepositions
sets.description: Prepositions like in, an, auf take accusative for directed motion (where to?) and dative for static location (where?). Same preposition, completely different meaning.

pairs.sentence_a: Sie legt das Buch auf den Tisch.
pairs.sentence_b: Das Buch liegt auf dem Tisch.
pairs.question: Which sentence describes where something already is?
pairs.explanation: "Auf den Tisch" (accusative) follows a motion verb (legen) — the book is being directed to the table. "Auf dem Tisch" (dative) follows a state verb (liegen) — the book is already there. Accusative answers "where to?"; dative answers "where?".

pairs.question: Which sentence describes being already inside the kitchen?
pairs.explanation: "In die Küche" (accusative) marks entry — direction of movement. "In der Küche" (dative) marks position — already inside. The verb (gehen vs. kochen) drives the choice, but you can use the case itself to check: if you're asking "where to?" use accusative; "where?" use dative.

sets.title: Konjunktiv II — Hypothetical & Polite
sets.description: Konjunktiv II marks statements as counterfactual or politely distanced from reality. It is the key register marker for hypothesis and soft requests in German.

pairs.sentence_a: Wenn ich Zeit habe, komme ich.
pairs.sentence_b: Wenn ich Zeit hätte, käme ich.
pairs.question: Which sentence implies the speaker probably won't have time?
pairs.explanation: "Habe / komme" (indicative) treats the time as a real possibility — if the condition is met, the visit happens. "Hätte / käme" (Konjunktiv II) distances the speaker from reality — the speaker signals the condition is unlikely or imaginary. Same structure, opposite epistemic stance.

pairs.question: Which version is more formal and polite?
pairs.explanation: "Können" (indicative) asks directly — it can feel abrupt. "Könnten" (Konjunktiv II) adds distance and deference — the speaker is asking hypothetically, which in German signifies politeness. This is the same mechanism as English "could you" vs "can you".
